name: Protein intake and IGF-1 mortality associations were strongly age-dependent
summary: Protein restriction supplies human IGF-1 relevance but also a major age boundary for translation.
statement: In NHANES III adults aged 50 and older, high protein intake was associated with higher overall and cancer mortality in ages 50 to 65 but with lower overall and cancer mortality after age 65, while mouse experiments linked protein and IGF-1 signaling to tumor progression.
population_or_model: Adults aged 50 years and older in NHANES III plus mouse experiments
time_horizon: Human mortality follow-up and complementary mouse tumor experiments
quantitative_note: For ages 50 to 65, the source reports a 75% increase in overall mortality and a fourfold increase in cancer death risk for high-protein intake; after age 65, high protein was associated with reduced overall and cancer mortality.
caveats: The human evidence is observational and exposure-defined, not a randomized protein-restriction intervention.
The age reversal is central and cautions against protein restriction in older adults without muscle, frailty, and nutrition safeguards.
Protein source, total calories, health status, and confounding can affect interpretation.
